abstract: Few behavioral phenomena are as ubiquitous as the speed-accuracy tradeoff (SAT). Observed across the animal kingdom—from insects and rodents to primates—the inverse relationship between decision speed and precision appears to be an inescapable property of choice behavior. Recently, the SAT has enjoyed a resurgence of interest as neuroscientific techniques begin to unravel its biological underpinnings and computational models increasingly treat it as a fundamental benchmark for decision-making. This paper provides a comprehensive overview of the SAT, beginning with a historical perspective on its role in shaping mathematical descriptions of cognitive processes. I then offer a "user’s guide" to SAT methodology, featuring a critical evaluation of common experimental manipulations and analytical techniques, alongside a discussion of the behavioral patterns that emerge when the tradeoff is directly controlled. Finally, I review the practical applications of these methodologies across several research domains.
